title: CASA0023 Remotely Sensing Cities and Environments LearnDiary
text:
Introduction
Hi! I am Yanxi, and my English name is Amelia. I majored in Landscape Gardening during my undergraduate studies and obtained a Bachelor’s degree in Agriculture. My coursework primarily focused on landscape design and plant studies.
After graduation, I traveled to several cities, which prompted me to reflect on how to understand urban environments in a more comprehensive and systematic way, rather than focusing solely on localized landscape design.
This website is my learning diary for the CASA0023 Remote Sensing Cities and Environment course in my second term, where I record my weekly summaries and personal reflections. The case study areas I will choose are divided into two types. The first type includes cities I am familiar with, such as Beijing and Taiyuan. The second type consists of cities I am particularly interested in, for instance, London and New York City.



A picture of an abandoned building next to an old park near my home


In principle, this would be the place to include a portrait of myself in the “Introduction” section, perhaps even a carefully composed photograph taken during my travels. Yet after some reflection, I decided instead to place an image of an old abandoned building near my home.
My curiosity about cities and landscapes may well have originated from these unfinished or abandoned structures. They stand remarkably close to the city center, adjacent to bustling commercial streets and parks frequented by local residents. During idle afternoons at home, I would often wander past such abandoned buildings. Gradually, I came to realize that what my undergraduate major—Landscape Gardening—could offer to a city was only one dimension of change. I began to ask broader questions: How might places like these be transformed to generate economic vitality or cultural resonance? What kinds of policies, planning decisions, and financial mechanisms would be required to make such transformations possible?
These questions pushed me beyond the boundaries of my original discipline. Confronted with numerous sites like these, I recognized the necessity of engaging with large-scale datasets and urban analytics in order to understand patterns, constraints, and opportunities at a systemic level. In many ways, this intellectual shift is the reason I am here today.
Although my current studies have expanded my perspective toward the macro scale of urban systems, I still find myself thinking about those aging, half-abandoned buildings in my small hometown. I place a photograph of one such structure here as a quiet acknowledgment of where everything began.

title: 2 Week2—Portfolio
section: 2.1.1 The production process of Quarto book
text:
2.1.1 The production process of Quarto book
(1)open a blank RStudio session -> New Project -> New Directory -> Quarto Book (2)generate file: index.qmd、intro.qmd、summary.qmd、reference.qmd、_quarto.yml (3)insert new chapter: new file -> quarto document -> insert into .yml document (4)render: terminal -> quarto preview

title: 3 Week3–Corrections
section: 3.1.1 Benefits of understanding the Correction Process
text:
3.1.1 Benefits of understanding the Correction Process

Research in the temporal dimension When comparing maps from different time periods, especially older datasets that do not have ARD versions, we may need to manually select ground control points and run linear regression to perform geometric correction. Understanding this process helps ensure that historical data can still be aligned and compared with newer datasets.
Selecting appropriate data When choosing datasets from official platforms, it is important to know what kinds of processing procedures the products have already undergone. This understanding helps us select data that is suitable for our research questions.
Transparency of data sources In academic research and reports, maintaining transparency about data sources is very important. Having a clear understanding of the correction process allows us to describe our data sources and preprocessing steps more clearly in our writing.

title: 3 Week3–Corrections
text:
3.1.2 Correction precautions

3.1.2.1 Avoid excessive correction
During the correction process, we should not blindly stack different methods together. Applying too many unnecessary corrections may damage the original characteristics of the data.


3.1.2.2 My understanding of the workflow
The order I organized here is slightly different from the order presented in class, but this is the workflow that makes the most sense to me:

Raw data (DN values) -> Radiometric calibration (DN -> Radiance) → Geometric correction -> Atmospheric correction (Radiance -> TOA Reflectance / Surface Reflectance) -> Orthorectification + Terrain correction



3.1.2.3 Changes in data types
(1)DN: the original digital number recorded by the sensor.
(2)Radiance: energy with physical units, which is influenced by the sensor, the atmosphere, and the position of the sun.
Note: Although the process from DN to Radiance removes the sensor gain and bias, spectral characteristics may still vary between different sensors.
(3)TOA Reflectance: removes sensor-related errors (eg: hardware noise and sensitivity differences) and the influence of solar conditions (eg: illumination intensity, angle, and distance).
(4)BOA / Surface Reflectance: further removes atmospheric effects (eg: path radiance, scattering, and atmospheric absorption) and some sensor-related effects, leaving mainly the intrinsic properties of the surface materials.
Note: Because real observations are also affected by terrain shadows and viewing geometry, what we sometimes obtain is actually apparent reflectance.


3.1.2.4 Order of corrections
It is important that atmospheric correction should be performed before terrain correction. Otherwise, atmospheric noise may be mistakenly treated as part of the ground signal and included in the terrain correction calculation, which can lead to systematic errors in the correction coefficients. When brightening shaded slopes. If atmospheric noise is amplified during this process, shaded areas may become excessively bright.
